past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 22
Mild mitral valve prolapse found incidentally on echocardiogram at age 26

medications.indication: Generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder

family_history: Mother with generalized anxiety disorder and depression
Maternal grandmother with dementia (type unknown), onset in late 70s
One sibling (brother, age 32) with migraine headaches
No known family history of epilepsy or seizure disorders

social_history.occupation: Elementary school teacher, currently on medical leave due to increasing episodes
social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, approximately 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any illicit drug use, past or present
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in an apartment, boyfriend visits frequently
social_history.exercise: Yoga three times weekly, previously ran 5K races but stopped due to episode-related anxiety
social_history.driving: Currently still driving but has become anxious about driving alone

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented. Mildly anxious in demeanor but cooperative. Speech fluent, no paraphasic errors. Naming 15/15 on Boston Naming Test sample items. Repetition and comprehension intact. Attention normal on digit span (7 forward, 5 backward). Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. Clock drawing normal. No evidence of thought disorder.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor, myoclonus, or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride and base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: During the interview, the patient paused briefly for approximately 5 seconds, appeared to swallow, and then resumed conversation seamlessly. When asked about the pause, she stated she felt a sudden wave of fear and nausea that passed quickly. She attributed it to anxiety.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden intense fear, palpitations, and a feeling of unreality, diagnosed as panic attacks but worsening despite treatment
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Delgado is a 29-year-old right-handed elementary school teacher who presents for inpatient evaluation of recurrent paroxysmal episodes that have been diagnosed as panic attacks over the past two years but have been escalating in frequency and are now occurring daily despite adequate treatment with sertraline and as-needed lorazepam. She describes the episodes as beginning with a sudden, overwhelming surge of fear that she describes as unlike normal anxiety, as though something terrible is about to happen but she cannot identify what. This is accompanied by a peculiar sensation she struggles to articulate, describing it variously as her stomach flipping, a wave of warmth rising from her abdomen to her chest, and occasionally a strange smell that she says is like burning rubber that nobody else around her can detect. During some episodes she experiences a profound feeling of depersonalization, as though the world around her has become unfamiliar and dreamlike.

The episodes typically last between 30 seconds and two minutes. Her boyfriend has witnessed several and reports that during the more intense ones, she stops speaking mid-sentence, stares vacantly, and appears to chew or smack her lips. She has no memory of these observed behaviors and initially denied they occurred until he recorded one on his phone. Following the more prolonged episodes she feels confused and exhausted for 10 to 20 minutes. She has never had a convulsive episode or lost consciousness in a dramatic fashion.

She was evaluated by her primary care physician who increased her sertraline from 50 mg to 100 mg and referred her to psychiatry. Her psychiatrist diagnosed refractory panic disorder and added lorazepam. A cardiologist evaluated her palpitations and attributed them to her known mitral valve prolapse and anxiety, obtaining a normal Holter monitor. Over the past three months, the episodes have increased from weekly to near-daily, occurring unpredictably during work, rest, and even during sleep on at least two occasions where her boyfriend observed her sitting up in bed, staring, and making repetitive hand movements. She has taken medical leave from teaching because an episode occurred in front of her students. She is now referred to neurology for further evaluation after her psychiatrist questioned whether these could be epileptic in nature.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness achieved with vertex waves; no stage II sleep captured

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions (T3, T4), left greater than right
findings.frequency: Intermittent, occurring in brief runs during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Arciform, monophasic, mu-like morphology at 6-11 Hz without aftergoing slow wave. Maximum amplitude 80 microvolts.
findings.clinical_correlation: Wicket spikes are a benign normal variant and should NOT be interpreted as epileptiform activity. Their temporal distribution can be misleading in the clinical context of suspected temporal lobe epilepsy.

findings.type: Small sharp spikes (benign epileptiform transients of sleep)
findings.morphology: Low amplitude (<50 microvolts), brief (<50 ms), without aftergoing slow wave or disruption of background

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, partially obscured temporal regions during wakefulness
QRS complexes visible in T3-T5 derivations, could be confused with sharp transients by less experienced readers

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. Mild symmetric background slowing, returned to baseline within one minute. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at mid-frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. Wicket spikes and small sharp spikes identified as benign normal variants. No definite epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute routine recording. Note: A normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy; sensitivity of a single routine EEG for interictal epileptiform discharges is approximately 29-55%. Prolonged or sleep-deprived EEG is recommended if clinical suspicion remains high.
eeg.limitations: Only brief drowsiness achieved, no sustained sleep recorded. Routine 30-minute recording duration limits sensitivity. Muscle artifact intermittently obscured temporal regions.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring if clinical suspicion for epilepsy remains
Sleep-deprived EEG may increase yield for interictal epileptiform discharges
Correlate with clinical semiology and neuroimaging

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.2 cm3 (35th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 3.3 cm3 (40th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No evidence of mesial temporal sclerosis
No cortical dysplasia or migrational abnormality identified
No mass lesion or pathological enhancement

Two non-specific white matter foci are of uncertain significance in a 29-year-old; differential includes migraine-related changes, incidental small vessel disease, or clinically isolated demyelination, though location and morphology are atypical for demyelination
mri.impression: MRI-negative study for epilepsy. No hippocampal sclerosis, mass lesion, or cortical malformation. Two small non-specific white matter foci of uncertain clinical significance, most likely incidental. Normal hippocampal volumes and symmetry.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low - lesions do not meet typical criteria for MS (location, morphology)

recommended_actions: Correlate with EEG and clinical findings
If epilepsy confirmed, consider 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol for subtle lesion detection
Non-specific white matter lesions do not require follow-up in isolation

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality. No features suggestive of cardiac syncope or channelopathy.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG. Combined with previously normal Holter monitor, a primary cardiac arrhythmia is unlikely as the etiology of the patient's episodes.

Additional.clinical_significance: Normal baseline prolactin. Note: specimen was drawn approximately 3 hours after the last reported episode, well outside the 10-20 minute window for detecting post-ictal elevation. A normal prolactin does not exclude epileptic seizures.
Positive for benzodiazepines consistent with prescribed lorazepam use. No other substances detected.

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel entirely within normal limits. No metabolic derangement to explain paroxysmal episodes. TSH normal. Prolactin within normal range but drawn well outside the post-ictal window, limiting its diagnostic utility. Urine drug screen positive for benzodiazepines, consistent with prescribed lorazepam. B12 and folate normal.
abnormal_values_summary: Urine drug screen positive for benzodiazepines - consistent with prescribed lorazepam, not clinically significant

background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles. All sleep stages recorded over 5-day monitoring.

findings.frequency: Rare during wakefulness (average 2 per hour), increasing during NREM sleep (average 8 per hour)
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, duration 50-70 ms, followed by aftergoing slow wave. Clear epileptiform morphology distinct from the wicket spikes seen on routine EEG.
findings.state: Predominantly NREM sleep, occasional during wakefulness
findings.clinical_correlation: Definite epileptiform discharges localizing to the right anterior temporal region. The rarity during wakefulness explains why routine EEG was normal.

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), remaining lateralized
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta activity evolving over 40 seconds, then attenuation, followed by right temporal 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta. Each event lasted 45-90 seconds. No bilateral spread.
findings.state: Two during wakefulness, one arising from sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Clinically, the patient reported sudden intense fear, epigastric sensation, and an olfactory hallucination (burning smell) during two events. During the third, she had behavioral arrest with lip smacking and right hand automatisms lasting approximately 60 seconds with brief post-ictal confusion. Semiology and EEG onset concordant with right mesial temporal lobe origin.

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric
findings.morphology: Similar right temporal onset pattern but with broader right hemispheric evolution over 90 seconds. One event showed possible brief left temporal involvement at termination.
findings.clinical_correlation: Patient became unresponsive during these events with oral and manual automatisms. Post-ictal confusion lasting 10-15 minutes. Semiology consistent with focal impaired awareness seizures of right temporal origin.

artifacts.impact: Partially obscured mid-ictal EEG but onset and early evolution clearly captured in all events

output.impression: Abnormal 5-day continuous video-EEG monitoring. Five habitual clinical seizures captured: three focal aware seizures (aura only with fear, epigastric rising, olfactory hallucination) and two focal impaired awareness seizures with behavioral arrest and automatisms. All seizures demonstrated unequivocal right anterior temporal electrographic onset at F8/T4. Rare right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges, maximal during sleep. These findings confirm the diagnosis of right temporal lobe epilepsy. The initially normal routine EEG is explained by the rarity of interictal discharges during wakefulness and the short recording duration.
output.limitations: No focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures captured during this monitoring period.
recommended_actions: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol to search for subtle right temporal structural lesion
Consider FDG-PET for additional lateralizing data
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy

findings.location: Right temporal pole and right mesial temporal cortex (right piriform cortex area)

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle blurring of the gray-white matter junction in the right temporal pole
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Subtle hyperintensity in the right temporal pole cortex, only visible on thin coronal cuts
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Faint hyperintensity in the right temporal pole, easily overlooked on standard sequences

signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Normal signal bilaterally on dedicated thin coronal cuts

findings.location: Right frontal subcortical and left parietal periventricular (previously identified)

additional_observations: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol including 3D T1 MPRAGE, thin coronal T2 and FLAIR (2 mm cuts perpendicular to long axis of hippocampus), and post-gadolinium sequences
Subtle right temporal pole abnormality was NOT visible on the prior 1.5T standard brain MRI
The gray-white junction blurring and cortical signal change raise suspicion for focal cortical dysplasia (type I or II) in the right temporal pole region
No hippocampal sclerosis. No dual pathology.
output.impression: 3T epilepsy protocol MRI reveals a subtle cortical abnormality in the right temporal pole with gray-white matter junction blurring and faint T2/FLAIR cortical hyperintensity, suspicious for focal cortical dysplasia. This was not visible on the prior standard 1.5T MRI. No hippocampal sclerosis. Previously noted non-specific white matter lesions are unchanged and remain incidental. The right temporal pole finding is concordant with the right temporal electrographic seizure onset on video-EEG.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Focal cortical dysplasia (type I or II)
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Moderate - gray-white blurring and cortical signal change in correct location
Low - no enhancement or clear mass, but cannot be entirely excluded
Low - no clinical history of prior insult to this region

recommended_actions: Correlate with video-EEG findings for concordance
FDG-PET to assess for right temporal hypometabolism
Consider post-processing with HARNESS-MRI or MRI morphometric analysis for quantitative assessment of suspected FCD

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Average verbal learning, suggesting intact left temporal function
Below average visual memory, potentially lateralizing to right temporal lobe dysfunction
Impaired visual memory consolidation, lateralizing to right temporal lobe dysfunction
Below average facial recognition, consistent with right temporal dysfunction

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates a material-specific visual memory deficit with impaired visual learning and consolidation, and below average facial recognition. Verbal memory is intact. This pattern lateralizes to right temporal lobe dysfunction and is concordant with right temporal electrographic seizure onset on video-EEG. High average overall intellectual functioning suggests adequate cognitive reserve. These findings support right temporal lobe epilepsy and may inform surgical planning.
abnormal_values_summary: Visual memory learning (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 37 - below average, lateralizing to right temporal dysfunction
Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 33 - impaired, consistent with right temporal lobe pathology
Facial recognition (Benton) T-score 35 - below average, consistent with right temporal dysfunction

findings.location: Right temporal pole and right mesial temporal structures

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Moderately reduced FDG uptake in the right temporal pole and right mesial temporal cortex. Mild hypometabolism extending into the right lateral temporal neocortex.
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal pole asymmetry index 0.82 (abnormal, normal >0.90)

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET performed during EEG-confirmed interictal state
Right temporal pole hypometabolism corresponds to the area of suspected focal cortical dysplasia on 3T MRI
No hypermetabolic foci to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates right temporal pole and mesial temporal hypometabolism concordant with the right temporal seizure onset zone on video-EEG and the subtle structural abnormality on 3T epilepsy protocol MRI. Multi-modal concordance supports right temporal lobe epilepsy, likely due to focal cortical dysplasia.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy with focal cortical dysplasia
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - concordant with EEG and MRI

recommended_actions: Multidisciplinary epilepsy surgery conference to review all data
Consider SEEG (stereoelectroencephalography) if non-invasive data deemed insufficient for surgical planning
Discuss surgical options including right temporal pole resection or tailored resection

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Patient is not currently on any antiepileptic medications. Her current medications include sertraline and as-needed lorazepam, neither prescribed as seizure prophylaxis. Lorazepam may have a partial protective effect as a benzodiazepine but is not appropriate monotherapy for focal epilepsy.

output.interpretation: No AED levels obtainable as patient is not on antiepileptic drug therapy. Initiation of a first-line AED for focal epilepsy is recommended now that the diagnosis has been confirmed by video-EEG monitoring. Recommended options include levetiracetam, lamotrigine, or lacosamide. Note that sertraline has no significant interactions with common AEDs, but the existing lorazepam use should be discussed in context of benzodiazepine tolerance and eventual taper planning.

output.query: temporal lobe epilepsy misdiagnosed panic disorder ictal fear

results.title: Ictal fear is a common semiology in temporal lobe epilepsy and is frequently misdiagnosed as panic disorder
results.summary: Ictal fear occurs in 10-20% of temporal lobe epilepsy patients and is the most common aura type in right temporal lobe seizures. The mean diagnostic delay when ictal fear is the predominant symptom is 7.4 years due to misdiagnosis as panic disorder.

results.title: Differentiating panic attacks from temporal lobe seizures
results.summary: Key distinguishing features include: seizure episodes are briefer (30-120 seconds vs 10-30 minutes for panic), stereotyped, associated with altered awareness, have post-ictal confusion, may occur from sleep, and often include olfactory or gustatory hallucinations. Panic attacks rarely have olfactory hallucinations and patients retain awareness.

results.title: MRI-negative focal epilepsy: prevalence, outcomes, and the role of advanced imaging
results.summary: Approximately 20-30% of focal epilepsy patients have normal standard MRI. 3T epilepsy protocol MRI, FDG-PET, and post-processing techniques can identify subtle lesions (especially focal cortical dysplasia) in up to 30% of previously MRI-negative cases.

output.summary: Temporal lobe epilepsy, particularly right-sided, is frequently misdiagnosed as panic disorder when ictal fear is the predominant aura. Key clinical clues include stereotyped episodes lasting under 2 minutes, occurrence from sleep, automatisms observed by witnesses, post-ictal confusion, and olfactory hallucinations. Normal routine EEG and MRI do not exclude temporal lobe epilepsy; prolonged video-EEG and 3T epilepsy protocol MRI significantly increase diagnostic yield. Mean diagnostic delay for temporal lobe epilepsy presenting as ictal fear is approximately 7 years.

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate to 200 mg daily over 8 weeks
interactions: Sertraline: no clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction, can be co-administered safely
Lorazepam: no significant interaction; however, both have CNS depressant effects, monitor for sedation
warnings: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis, especially with rapid titration. Must titrate slowly (25 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 50 mg daily x 2 weeks, then increase by 50 mg every 1-2 weeks)
Risk of serious rash is higher in women and in patients under 30 (this patient has both risk factors)
Patient has penicillin allergy (hives) - not a cross-reactivity concern but warrants close monitoring of any rash during titration
Oral contraceptives can reduce lamotrigine levels by approximately 50% - assess contraceptive use
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Levetiracetam 250 mg twice daily, titrate to 500-1000 mg twice daily (faster titration, no rash risk, but higher risk of psychiatric side effects - relevant given patient's anxiety history)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 150-200 mg twice daily (well-tolerated, fewer psychiatric effects)
Oxcarbazepine 150 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily (risk of hyponatremia, check sodium)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right temporal lobe epilepsy, MRI-negative on standard imaging, with suspected focal cortical dysplasia on 3T epilepsy protocol)

differential.diagnosis: Panic disorder with refractory panic attacks
differential.key_features: Pre-existing anxiety disorder makes this a compelling initial diagnosis. However, episodes are stereotyped, brief (30-120 seconds), include olfactory hallucinations and automatisms not typical of panic, occur from sleep, and are followed by confusion. Video-EEG with captured events definitively distinguishes the two.

Psychiatric comorbidity (anxiety, panic disorder) is a risk factor for PNES. However, captured events on video-EEG show clear right temporal electrographic seizure onset concordant with clinical semiology, excluding PNES for these specific events.

Known mitral valve prolapse could raise suspicion for cardiac syncope. However, episodes do not involve loss of postural tone, normal ECG and Holter, and retained consciousness during many events argues against syncope. Palpitations in this context are likely autonomic seizure manifestations.
Patient is young with no vascular risk factors. TIAs cause negative symptoms and do not present with positive phenomena such as fear, olfactory hallucinations, or automatisms.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history of episode semiology from patient and witnesses, including duration, stereotypy, associated phenomena (olfactory hallucinations, automatisms), occurrence from sleep, and post-episode confusion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stereotyped brief episodes with ictal fear, epigastric rising, olfactory hallucination, automatisms witnessed, post-ictal confusion, and occurrence from sleep - features atypical for panic disorder

optimal_actions.action: Review video recorded by boyfriend showing episode with behavioral arrest and automatisms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Objective evidence of altered awareness and motor automatisms inconsistent with panic attacks
Normal interictal exam but observe for subtle clinical events during encounter
Normal EEG with benign variants (wicket spikes) that must not be over-interpreted as epileptiform

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy and proceed to prolonged video-EEG monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Captured habitual events with right anterior temporal electrographic seizure onset confirming temporal lobe epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain (initially standard, then 3T epilepsy protocol when diagnosis confirmed)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard MRI normal (MRI-negative epilepsy); 3T epilepsy protocol reveals subtle right temporal pole FCD

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, and timed prolactin if possible
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic workup; prolactin uninformative if drawn outside post-ictal window

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, considering psychiatric comorbidities
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lamotrigine or lacosamide preferred given anxiety comorbidity; levetiracetam acceptable but higher psychiatric side effect risk

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on diagnosis, driving restrictions, and seizure safety
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed that episodes are epileptic seizures, not panic attacks; driving restrictions discussed

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for further workup including neuropsychological testing and FDG-PET
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral for epilepsy surgery evaluation given MRI-visible lesion on high-resolution imaging

critical_actions: Recognize that stereotyped brief episodes with olfactory hallucinations, automatisms, and post-ictal confusion are atypical for panic disorder and warrant epilepsy evaluation
Do not accept a normal routine EEG as excluding epilepsy; proceed to prolonged video-EEG monitoring
Do not over-interpret wicket spikes on routine EEG as epileptiform discharges
Confirm diagnosis with video-EEG capturing habitual events with electrographic correlate
Obtain 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol for MRI-negative focal epilepsy
Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug therapy once diagnosis is confirmed
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing panic disorder solely based on psychiatric history and normal routine EEG without pursuing video-EEG monitoring
Increasing sertraline or adding another psychiatric medication to treat presumed refractory panic disorder without considering epilepsy
Over-interpreting wicket spikes or benign EEG variants as epileptiform to prematurely diagnose epilepsy without capturing events
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold
Attributing the non-specific white matter lesions on MRI to the cause of seizures (red herring)
Performing lumbar puncture without clinical indication for CNS infection or inflammation
Allowing continued driving without discussing seizure-related driving restrictions
key_reasoning_points: Ictal fear is the most common aura type in right temporal lobe epilepsy and is frequently misdiagnosed as panic disorder, with mean diagnostic delays of approximately 7 years

Key features distinguishing temporal lobe seizures from panic attacks include: stereotyped episodes lasting under 2 minutes, olfactory hallucinations, witnessed automatisms with impaired awareness, occurrence from sleep, and post-ictal confusion
A normal routine EEG has limited sensitivity (29-55%) for detecting interictal epileptiform discharges; prolonged video-EEG monitoring is the definitive diagnostic test
Wicket spikes are benign EEG variants commonly found in the temporal regions that can be mistaken for epileptiform sharp waves, particularly in the context of suspected temporal lobe epilepsy
Approximately 20-30% of focal epilepsy is MRI-negative on standard imaging; 3T epilepsy protocol MRI can reveal subtle focal cortical dysplasia not visible on standard studies
The non-specific white matter lesions are red herrings that could mislead toward a demyelinating diagnosis; their location and morphology do not explain temporal lobe seizures
The pre-existing psychiatric diagnosis of panic disorder with anxiety creates a strong diagnostic anchoring bias that must be overcome through systematic analysis of episode semiology
Choice of AED should consider psychiatric comorbidities; levetiracetam can worsen anxiety and irritability, making lamotrigine or lacosamide potentially preferable first-line agents

metadata.author: NeuroBench case generator
metadata.estimated_complexity: Diagnostic puzzle - misleading initial presentation as panic disorder with normal initial EEG and MRI, multiple red herrings
red_herrings: Pre-existing psychiatric diagnoses (GAD, panic disorder) anchoring toward psychiatric etiology
Normal routine EEG with benign variants (wicket spikes) that could be misinterpreted
Normal standard MRI with incidental non-specific white matter lesions
Known mitral valve prolapse and palpitations suggesting cardiac etiology
Normal prolactin level drawn outside post-ictal window
Positive urine drug screen for benzodiazepines (prescribed lorazepam)
